methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Seed → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.5x) to $2M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Robotics & Manufacturing (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 11 same-sector peers exist in Robotics & Manufacturing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
Orbital Composites is a seed-stage robotics and manufacturing company founded by Cole Nielsen that has raised $6.6M to date. The valuation analysis draws on a comp universe of nine private robotics firms and one public comparable, though meaningful peer-median analysis proved limited by the scarcity of identically-staged companies in this sector.

The stage-progression method was the primary applicable approach. Using total raised ($6.6M) as a proxy for seed-stage value and applying a standard 2.5x step-up multiplier to next stage yields an implied valuation of $6M. This approach assumes typical venture progression dynamics and reflects the company's position relative to seed-to-Series A advancement patterns. The peer-median method could not be reliably applied; while 11 same-sector robotics companies exist in the comp set, fewer than three met narrow filtering criteria (same stage, adjacent stage, or similar valuation tier), making a median-based derivation unrepresentative.

The triangulated analysis supports a valuation range of $5M to $7M, with a median point of $6M. This narrow range reflects reliance on a single methodological input rather than true triangulation, which introduces material uncertainty around the valuation. Key sensitivities include the appropriateness of the 2.5x multiplier for this specific company's risk profile and competitive positioning, capital efficiency relative to peer benchmarks, and the timing assumptions embedded in stage-progression models. Investors should weight this estimate accordingly given the limited methodological corroboration.
